definition: NATO's core aging members face a double bind: rising security threats requiring higher defense spending AND aging-driven rising social spending competing for the same shrinking fiscal envelope. Younger EM partners have incompatible priorities shaped by their demographic position. This creates structural security architecture friction exploitable by great power competitors.
evidence: EU average defense spending ~1.5% GDP must increase 0.5-1.5pp
Age-related spending increases of 2-4pp GDP over 15 years
Younger EM partners (India, Indonesia) prioritize job creation and infrastructure
Germany's Zeitenwende shows security threats can override fiscal constraints
open_questions: Can security threats sustainably override fiscal constraints in aging democracies?
How do divergent demographic positions within alliances affect burden-sharing?
Will the friction create exploitable opportunities for adversaries?
